title: Git Flow (& alternatives)
objectives: Compare with Trunk-Based and GitHub Flow
Choose a branching strategy for your team

sections.markdown: A **branching strategy** defines how teams manage feature work, releases and hotfixes in git. Classic **Git Flow** uses long-lived develop + release branches; **GitHub Flow** and **Trunk-Based Development** favor short-lived branches merging to a single main.

sections.code:
Mobile-friendly Trunk-Based + release branches:
  main                (always deployable)
   ├── feat/x         (short-lived, days)
   ├── feat/y         (short-lived, days)
   └── release/8.1    (cut from main; only fixes cherry-picked back)

  Feature flags gate incomplete work in main.

sections.markdown: Modern high-velocity teams don't use Git Flow — the long-lived `develop` branch adds friction. They use Trunk-Based with **feature flags** to hide unfinished work + short-lived branches merged within a day. Git Flow still fits SDK/versioned-library or on-prem-release cycles.
Long-lived feature branches → nightmare merges. If a branch lives >1 week, break it up. If features aren't ready, gate them behind flags in main.
For mobile apps: main (deployable) + short-lived feature branches + a release/x.y branch cut per release. Cherry-pick hotfixes to the release branch, tag store submissions, keep main moving.

flashcards.back: All work integrates to main via short-lived branches (usually < 1 day).
Painful merges and integration risk — gate incomplete features with flags instead.
Trunk-based + a release/x.y branch cut per store submission; cherry-pick hotfixes.

quiz.q: Which is favored by modern high-velocity teams?
quiz.explain: Short-lived branches + flags scale better with CD.

options: main + release/x.y branches for store cuts
quiz.explain: Cut a release branch to stabilize while main keeps moving.

challenges.text: Set up a mobile-friendly branching model: main + release/x.y.
Cherry-pick a hotfix from main into an active release branch.
Introduce feature flags to hide an incomplete refactor in main.
Migrate a Git Flow repo to Trunk-Based without a re-history.

interviewQuestions: Git Flow vs GitHub Flow vs Trunk-Based?
When does Git Flow still make sense?
How do feature flags relate to branching?
How do you manage mobile release branches?
How do you avoid painful merges?
